speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 19, 1996. We are with Cela Sarna. This is tape three. Mrs. Sarna, you were just arriving in Majdanek at the camp. When you arrived, what did you see? Were you near a town, where there were other people around?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was no town. We didn't see it. There was a place where the-- where the train stopped and this was already there, Majdanek. And we were taken off all the trains and right away selected. Like I said, the women separate, the men separate, the children separate, all the people separate. And we started-- we didn't know what to expect. But we were taken to a fifth field where only women were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who was doing the selection? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of their names? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. If-- if you see a German in Majdanek, you are not alive anymore. So they took us on the fifth field. This was the woman field. And we went in, like I said, to like a long alley-- alley? Alley, what you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was little rooms like it looked like horses were standing there. And they started saying, take off the-- the dress, take of the-- first the shoes with the stockings, then the dress, then the bra, then the pants until we came on the other side was staying Germans laughing. Maybe you saw it in Majdanek, people, every one of us walked out this way because we were naked. And they gave us to drink a sweet coffee. It was so sweet probably I didn't see it while the war was on. We all drank the coffee because we were thirsty, that's the first thing we had. And it was the little package and everybody-- every one of us took a package. This was a dress. We put it on. A small girl, a big girl-- everybody later exchanged among themselves. I was very skinny so whatever I put on was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these all-- the-- the Germans there who were laughing, they were just men there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a shower, was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: --cleaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not now. So we had the dresses and they took us to the place where we gonna sleep. There were big-- a lot of barracks. And in the barracks were cots with a-- a-- like a blanket on every-- on every one of them. And we went in, we took our place wherever they told us. And we realize this is just for women. In this place was already women a little bit further down. And when we already came in, then they took us to take a shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the barrack-- how many people, how many beds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 500 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And it was like three bunk beds one on the-- on the other. Nothing just-- just the boards, just the boards. And since we were very skinny with me and my knees, we decided we'll take the third one, so we went on the top. And you really couldn't sit up. You had to lay because it wasn't that high in three-- in three bunk beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day I tried to get up a little bit, and I noticed that a board is empty, the board is picking up. It's not nailed down. I said, oh, I see the board. And nobody figured if we have to run away, we'll go underneath the board. Anyway, they took us later to [NON-ENGLISH] to take a bath. We took a bath and we walked in, I cannot explain it to you. It's like a big shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's as big-- twice as big as this house. It was showers shower and a tub. And it didn't take too long once one was screaming, this is gas, it's not water, and we all started screaming. And it wasn't even gas, it was water. We took a shower, everybody was screaming, and then we came out. And we had nothing to-- to wipe ourselves. And we put on the gown what they gave us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you underwear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. From then on, you became no human, just-- just like dehumanized. That's it. And we came back to the barracks and everybody-- it's not nice even to talk when the man is sitting-- everybody had an urge that you had to go to the bathroom. And it was in a field. It was little like a-- a-- you went to the bathroom, then you had to cover it with a-- with a spoon or whatever was laying there. And we looked at each other, every one of us got their period in the same second. This was the-- the hot, sweet water what they gave us. Every one of us, all the girls, we looked at each other, we were all bleeding. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. What can you do? There was-- there was paper laying there. We took the paper and it take-- took only three hours and we all didn't get it anymore. And I didn't get it until I was after the war. [SIGHS] And we went in to sleep. It was already evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- not us. We didn't cut it, no. We cut-- we-- we all wear short hair because in the-- in the ghetto it was hard to keep it clean, too. So we all had short hair. No, we didn't-- they didn't cut our hair. We went in to sleep and we probably slept all night. It was 4 o'clock in the morning. They woke us all up with-- with [NON-ENGLISH] We shall all come out. And we stayed in the appell they call it from 4 o'clock till maybe 9 o'clock at night-- not at night, in the morning. And they were just going over and like selecting whom they don't need it, who was too old, who was already tired. They shoot a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: They shot them right there?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right there. And they-- right away, they had prepared people with [INAUDIBLE] and they took you away. This was men. Now I'll say we laid on the beds and those two girls decided they are from the same town, there were two sisters, they're gonna run away. This was maybe already two weeks we were there. In the morning, we get a little bit of coffee and a piece of bread. I ate from a pound, from-- from a two pounds, maybe. And people were stealing you because you had to watch the piece of bread. People were so hungry that they stoled yours. Not because they were-- they wanted, but it's such a slow death when you are hungry that it's hard to explain. It's like something-- somebody is sitting inside and growing and growing. It's-- It's just-- it's not to live and it's not to die. And they decided to run away. I don't know how they did it because-- but they knew they were from the same town so they knew every corner of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The town where Majdanek was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they ran out. Those Polish people what she was saying turned them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What Polish people were these? They lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish-- Polish, they lived there. Yeah. They turned them in and they brought them back. They made an appell. They took off all their clothes, and they hung them in the middle of this camp. And how many-- maybe a few thousand people, we had to stay for two days. Not only those two-- I forgot to tell you-- but the people were the one on this side and one on this side. That means you are your brother's keepers. If somebody runs away next to you, you are responsible. So they hung four girls, and we stayed for two days like this and watched them hanging naked. This was the worst thing in our lives. And they took us out for walks sometimes. [SIGHS] We were-- we carried stones, what they needed. In the morning we took [? him ?] in one place and tomorrow they decided they don't need them so we took [? him ?] in another-- another place. But this is a thing what I have to tell you. I never saw anybody. I didn't know that anybody's alive. One day--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your family, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One day-- and they separated us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, they said-- one day came a girl and she said, you know, your brother is alive. We saw him. But you better don't go see him because he is a skeleton. And he is on the fourth field. He is bringing those stones. So I said, my brother? I'll go with the same stones on this place. And we went. No, I decided I'm not gonna eat my bread this day, and I'm gonna give him the bread. I think this is very hard for me to say. And we came, and he was on the 'nother side. If you see a skeleton really, nothing really bones was hanging from him. And I took the piece of bread, and I threw it to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was afraid like somebody would take it from me. And he did not chew it. He like-- he like put it in his mouth and-- and took it down. And I didn't see that two German girls because we had ladies that time already. They watched me, and they called me over, what did you do? And I told them in German, this is [SPEAKING GERMAN] and [? I ?] gave him my bread. And they started beating me up. I was swollen all over, especially my behind. What did they use?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN], what do you call it? A [GERMAN] in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: A stick, a club?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: A pipe? A whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: A whip. Right, a whip. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I couldn't even walk back to the barracks, but with all my strength I walked back. I was all swollen. And there and then I decided, I said, you know what? Tomorrow morning-- I cannot go there to the appell, because I cannot even walk. I remember about this board, and they pushed me, a few of the girls from my town, they pushed me upstairs. And I laid there for two days. And they really wanted to give me some water, but the water was closed for all day. We could not go in the place where we have the water, just in the morning for an hour. And the Germans was standing and hitting us, so a lot of people didn't even go in for the water. I myself didn't. So they-- one of us was a little bit taller. He says, you know what's good? We'll give you urine, and you'll put it on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And believe it or not, they gave me wet cloths of urine, and I put it on the body. And for two days I was laying and on the third day, I felt much better. They gave me the bread what had belonged to me, they gave me-- they knew about me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you were missing from the appell?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't count us really by the appell. They just looked. They made believe they counted us. They're gonna count it was for a couple of thousand people, but they made believe. They looked and they counted. So I didn't miss-- they didn't miss me. And every day people were dying. How could they know? People died 20 a day, 30 a day and a lot of them hung themself up by the-- by the window, a lot of them. You had to be lucky to come to the window, too. But I didn't hung myself, really. [? Even with this-- ?] in this Majdanek, we went all like this for seven months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After seven months, they took us all together. I think by that time was the half already what they brought us. People died. People were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was gas chambers, but I never-- I never was there. If I would be there, I wouldn't come out. It was. Those older people and the men and the children, they were gassed. Twice-- twice a week we used to go to take a bath, to this place, to the same place where we came. One day we came, this was already very short before they took us away. I saw bringing Russian soldiers. It's just like you see [? bells ?] coming down, that's how they pushed them down right in the gas chambers. It was in the same place where we took the thing, but they probably had a place where they put the gas on. And they-- after seven months, they get us all together and they-- we thought that they're finished, they take us to finish. But one thing we knew, if they want to finish us, they-- they have a place where to finish us off. We-- we-- they put us again on trains and the same day we arrived to Skarzysko.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with? Were you-- you mentioned your niece.

speaker: interviewee
text: My niece. She was with me from the minute I came back from the jumping the train until I-- until we got liberated. Everybody knew that she is my sister because I figured if they knew that's my sister, they won't separate us. We came to Skarzysko, we saw people dressed, men and women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't believe it. It was like coming from-- from hell to heaven. It was a lot better. [SIGHS] This was a camp-- a working camp. And again were selections. The younger people, the men, and a lot of them, they didn't want it. They asked us if anybody knows how to sew a machine. And I said, yes, I know how to do with my hands. I said to my niece, you say it, too. She didn't know it, but I said, I'll teach you. I don't know-- I thought they were going to sew something. Anyway, it was-- this was a working camp. They made ammunition. They gave us clothes, we got right away something to eat. There was [? preachers. ?] But there were like 50 people in one barrack or 100 people-- no, 50 people, I think, was in one barrack. It was only two, one-- one bunk bed and one on the top. There was already mattress made from straw. And we had new cots. They gave us, [SIGHS] like-- like-- I don't know. I don't know how to explain it, but like exactly what it was from coming there to here. And in the morning, they took us to the factory. And there were Polish people. They were working in the factory and they showed us what to do. And every one of them learned very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish people who were working there were not prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They came in the morning and they went back at night. But they were like meisters already. They were like-- they showed us what to do. They were like [? elders ?] for us, like a boss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know-- did they know who you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure they knew. They knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask you what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't. They knew everything what happened, believe me. And we were working in this factory. We also had-- ate [? four ?] bread in-- in-- and every day we got soup, soup from-- from cabbage soup. And even the Polish people got cabbage soup in the camp, in the factory. And once they were so bad, the cabbage soup, that they didn't want to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every one of them gave a little bit to us. Believe me, I started looking forward. Soon I ate up this cabbage soup, I looked forward for the next Wednesday because I knew that next Wednesday it'll give me the soup. And we lived there, it wasn't too bad there. I mean, they killed every day. We were afraid-- we had to do [INAUDIBLE], we had to do so much and so much, and they had to be done right. But you learned how to do it, and you did your [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: You worked very, very hard. There was already [? colonization, ?] and there was already water. And-- and the men were-- lived in different barracks. The women lived in different barracks but we worked in the same-- the same place. We worked there I think not to long. We worked there maybe six months, seven months. I don't know exactly, but this was already '44. And then probably the Russians starting-- started going. There was already the war between them and they have-- they had to close up this factory and they opened it up in Tschenstochau and they took us all to Tschenstochau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trains, everything by trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of train was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything the same trains. It was cattle trains. But I-- in every-- in every camp you had policemen Jewish-- Jewish policemen and-- and Jewish [? officers. ?] And they told us that they're taking us to Tschenstochau, there is another factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me more about the Jewish policemen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were all rotten. They were really rotten-- not-- not all of them, really, but some of them were so rotten, terrible. If you didn't do your [INAUDIBLE], they-- they beat you and they-- and if you didn't go so fast and if you didn't come out from the barracks so fast. They were always also had those things in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whips, yeah. And a lot-- and there was-- in Skarzysko was a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [INAUDIBLE]. He was the German. He was well-known. They used to say that he takes out girls, the nicest-looking girls from Skarzysko. Well, they came from Skarzysko and they weren't really so run down like we came. And they used to take them in later 10 at the time and kill them. That's what they say. I never saw it, but I saw one hanging in Skarzysko. There was one boy and he took off from the machine belt and they-- and-- and they caught him. So they took him into the barracks and they hang him. And again, we stayed all day and watched it. And we had to see if you steal something then-- then you're going to get it. Once a week we used to go to take a bath, to a bath house. Skarzysko wasn't the worst camp. So they took us, and they took us to Tschenstochau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- I think it was in '44, the beginning of '44, because-- maybe March of '44. I don't know exactly. I don't know any of those dates. But I remember I came to Tschenstochau and again, I worked by the same machine, but we had a terrible meister. He gave us-- he gave us the work to work double what we did. So we learned in the day while we could, we did a little bit more and would put it away. [SIGHS] And we were all young. In Tschenstochau-- in Skarzysko I was doing-- I was knitting. I made a sweater for meister-- for one, not the German, for the Polish. And he used to give me a piece of bread. He used to give me on Wednesdays soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the wool?

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought the wool. He brought the wool and I made it and I-- and I gave him. I-- I finished the sweater. I was up all night, and I finished the sweater. Then he wanted some gloves, so he brought me the knitting-- knitting-- two knittings and he bought me five knittings. Already I didn't give them back. And he brought me wool.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the same night, they took us away to Tschenstochau. So when I came to Tschenstochau, I had five knitting things, and I had a lot of wool. So I figured, I'll start making a sweater or whatever is for me. So I was sitting in there after work, I was sitting and knitting outside. Came a-- a boy to me-- and this is my husband-- and he said to me, would you knit for me a sweater? I have two old sweaters, and I would like to make one. I'll pay you for it with bread. I mean, we didn't have any money. I said, why not? And he bought me out two sweaters, two old sweaters. They were so dirty. [SIGHS] I took them apart, and I started knitting a sweater for-- for him. And he gave me-- every so often he bought me a piece of bread until he saw the finished work, he gave me a half a bread. And he started coming every evening. We talked about it, and it was-- Yom Kippur. He sings beautiful. And he sang me 'Kol Nidre.' Tell me about the living conditions there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when-- when you first arrived? Was there also the selection and the showers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was-- no, no. They didn't have it, but it was a selection. Who was older that they took it away. We didn't see them killing, but they took it away, and we never saw them again. And they killed-- they also killed. They came out in the morning, if they didn't like how you stood, there was appells too, but not bad. Before we went into the factory, but you stood in groups because there was a lot of places different people went.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your now husband sang 'Kol Nidre' for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to observe any holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were sitting on a-- No. I didn't even know it was Yontif. He said it for some reason. He said that somebody told him that today is 'Kol Nidre.' And we didn't observe anything. I didn't know from anything. We were-- you can't imagine. We were people without hope. We didn't need anything. We didn't have anything. Our dream was to live through the war and sit down with our bread and eat how much I wanted and later I can die. This was my dream. I don't care if I don't live anymore, but just to sit down by a bread and [? metal ?] knife and cut how much I wanted. I'll make it faster. We lived in Tschenstochau. We worked by this-- in this factory for long-- for quite over the winter. It was cold. They used to come and see if we have-- if we are clean. We had-- and if we were not clean, they used to take us out in the winter in the cold and-- and beat us. It was hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you weren't clean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clean. If-- you know how it is. How-- how could we be clean? You had only one dress and you wore it in the night. In the night you took it off and from the coat you made a-- a pillow. And the shoes you took off, and you put it under the pillow-- under the coat so you have it a little higher. This was our conditions. Food we didn't have, we ate very little. And water we had a lot. This was-- we could wash really every day. This we had because we had to go into the factory. So we washed every day and we on the same dress. None of us had underpants, none of us had a bra or nothing. Just the dress and the coat and the shoes. And this was wintertime already. It was fall. And that's how the days went by. It came the end of Tschenstochau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let me ask you. You said that you were able to speak to your husband sometimes in the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: After work. It was a big--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened after work when they brought you back?

speaker: interviewee
text: After work, we could walk out and see the-- there was benches, like little places. And we could sit there and talk. There-- there were no Germans really then inside, unless they came in from outside. I have to tell you one thing. It's finished. I have a brother-in-law. This what you see, he looks like my brother, but he was like my husband. He is the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where I worked was a big place where they had the steel for the-- for the ammunition. And for some reason, he got two-- three potatoes from our meister. And he came and he said, I have three potatoes. Can you put it in for me and cook it? This was if you put it into this oven, it took a second and you took it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you come in later with the potatoes, I'll cook 'em for you. And I-- I was-- he already knew me from my husband. I'll cook them for you. And he bought, it was a little boy, he is a [INAUDIBLE]. So he's very young. And he bought it in, the three potatoes. And I put in the three potatoes in the oven, and believe it or not I couldn't take them out because the meister came in, the German came in, and I had to let 'em burn. And he came for the three potatoes, and I had to tell him that I don't have 'em, they got burnt. And he start crying, you ate them up! They were my potatoes. You ate 'em up. Till two-- till this day, he doesn't believe me that I didn't eat 'em up.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think I'll stop now and change the tape.
